---
title: "Week 6: Writing Functions"
author: "Joe Nese"
institute: "University of Oregon<br>Spring 2026"
format:
  revealjs: 
    theme: default
    highlight-style: github
    incremental: false
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 3.5
    scrollable: true
    slide-number: true
execute: 
  echo: true
  warning: false
embed-resources: true
---

```{r}
#| include: false

library(tidyverse)

theme_set(theme_minimal(20))
update_geom_defaults("point", list(size = 3))
```

# Writing Functions

**Week 6**

##

![](https://media1.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExZjZsc3ozNW03dHRmYnFvN3U3azJ2ZzUyZGUxeGF5ZGxlN2lpOG5ydiZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/3oKIPag5cpeU0u0Su4/giphy.gif)

##

![](./images/artemis_1.webp)


## Agenda {.smaller}

* Review Midterm
* Everything is a function
* Components of a function
* Function workflows

**Learning objectives**

* Understand and be able to fluently refer to the three fundamental components of a function
* Understand the workflows that often lead to writing functions, and how you iterate from interactive work to writing a function
* Be able to write a few basic functions 

# Review Midterm

# Functions

## Functions

Anything that carries out an operation in `R` is a function

. . .

For example

```{r plus-infix1}
3 + 5
```

. . .

The `+` is a function (what's referred to as an *infix* function)

- where a function name (or symbol) appears between its arguments, rather than before `function(arg1, arg2)`

. . .

Any ideas on how we could re-write the above to make it look more "function"-y?


## Try this 

```{r plus-fun}
`+`(3, 5) 
 
``` 

. . .

What's going on here?

## What's going on here? {.smaller}

The `+` operator is a function that takes two arguments (both numeric), which it sums

We're explicitly calling the `+` function with two arguments: `+(arg1, arg2)`

. . .

This is true for all `R` operators

```{r}
`*`(4, 6)
```

. . .

```{r}
`^`(9, 2)
```

. . .

```{r}
`>`(5, 3)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
x <- c("a", "b", "c")
`[`(x, 2)
```


## Even true for `<-`

What's going on here?

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
`<-`(a, 10)
a
```

. . .

Everything is a function!

## Challenge

How would we write this in the same manner?

```{r plus-infix2}
3 + 5 + 7
```

. . .

```{r, plus-fun2}
`+`(7, `+`(3, 5))
```

. . .

or

```{r, plus-fun3}
`+`(3, 5) %>% 
	`+`(7)
```

. . .

but not the native pipe!

```{r, plus-fun4}
#| error: true
`+`(3, 5) |>  
	`+`(7)
```

## Want to know why?

![](https://media0.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExb2UzaWZ5Y2MzbGhqY2VzanhlN3BxMmp3dmcwNTR2M2g3eW1ybnhzZyZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/G5X63GrrLjjVK/giphy.gif)

## I knew you would {.smaller}

`%>%` 

- takes the LHS and constructs a new function call, inserting the LHS as the first argument
- so `3 %>% + (5)` becomes `+(3, 5)`

. . .

`|>`

- requires the RHS to be a syntactically valid function call
- meaning it must look like `f(...)` with a proper identifier as the function name
- `3 |> + (5)` sees a backtick-quoted name, not a proper function

## Mean trick

Want to introduce a devious bug? Redefine `+`

`runif()` generates random deviates from the `unif`orm distribution ($min$ = 0, $max$ = 1)

```{r bug}
`+` <- function(x, y) {
	if(runif(1) < 0.01) {
		sum(x, y) * -1
	} else {
		sum(x, y)
	}
}
```

## Mean trick

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
tibble(
  x = 1:500,
  y = 1:500
) |> 
  mutate(sum = map2_dbl(x, y, `+`)) |> 
  filter(sum < 0)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
table(map2_dbl(1:500, 1:500, `+`) < 0)
```

## Undo

```{r }
rm(`+`, envir = globalenv())
table(map2_dbl(1:500, 1:500, `+`) < 0)
```

## Functions as objects 

Functions are also (usually) objects!

```{r, lm-a}
a <- lm
a(hp ~ drat + wt, data = mtcars)
```

## What does this all mean?

* Anything that carries out **ANY** operation in `R` is a function

* Functions are generally, but not always, stored in an object 
    + otherwise known as binding the function to a name

![](https://media1.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExcGRwejF2czhrcGkzNmNyOXRxYW12aXlya2R4am1hZXJ3emYwMjFrcyZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/ji6zzUZwNIuLS/giphy.gif)


## Revisit anonymous functions

::: {.incremental}
- The function for computing the mean is bound to the name `mean`
- When running things through loops, you may often want to apply a function *without* binding it to a name
- An anonymous function
:::

## Example

```{r anonymous-fun}
vapply(mtcars, function(x) length(unique(x)), FUN.VALUE = double(1))
```

. . .

`length(unique())` is not a known function

## Another possibility {.smaller}

* If you have a bunch of functions, you might consider storing them all in a list
* You can then access the functions in the same way you would subset any list

. . .

```{r fun-list}
funs <- list(
  quarter = function(x) x / 4,
  half = function(x) x / 2,
  double = function(x) x * 2,
  quadruple = function(x) x * 4
)
```

. . .

Strange, but works

```{r fun-list-eval}
funs$quarter(100)
funs[["half"]](100)
funs[[4]](100)
```

## What are the implications?

If we can store functions in a vector (list), then we can loop through the vector just like any other!

```{r smry-list}
smry <- list(
  n = length, 
  n_miss = function(x) sum(is.na(x)),
  n_valid = function(x) sum(!is.na(x)),
  mean = mean, 
  sd = sd
)
```

. . .

```{r smry-loop1}
#| output-location: fragment
map_dbl(smry, ~.x(mtcars$mpg))
```

## Careful though

This doesn't work

```{r smry-loop1-fail}
#| error: true
map_dbl(smry, mtcars$mpg)
```

## Why not?

Remember what `{purrr}` does

![](https://d33wubrfki0l68.cloudfront.net/f0494d020aa517ae7b1011cea4c4a9f21702df8b/2577b/diagrams/functionals/map.png)

## With `map_df()`

```{r map_df_smry}
#| output-location: fragment
map_df(smry, ~.x(mtcars$mpg))
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
map_df(smry, ~.x(mtcars$cyl))
```

## Challenge {.smaller}

What if we wanted this for all columns?

We need to make a nested loop (loop each function through each column)

This is hard!

. . .

```{r smry-all-cols}
#| output-location: fragment
map_df(mtcars, function(col) map_df(smry, ~.x(col)),
       .id = "column")
```

. . .

`col` is an explicitly named function argument of the outer `map_df()` so there is no ambiguity with the `~` from the inner `map_df()`

## Easier / more readable

Avoid nested loops!

Instead, turn (at least) one of the loops into a function

```{r summarize_col}
summarize_col <- function(column) {
  map_df(smry, ~.x(column))
}
```

. . .

Now we can just loop this function through each column

```{r all-cols}
#| output-location: fragment
map_df(mtcars, summarize_col, .id = "column")
```

## Wrap the whole thing in a function

```{r df-function}
summarize_df <- function(df) {
  map_df(df, summarize_col, .id = "column")
}
```

. . .

```{r summarize-airquality}
summarize_df(airquality)
```


## Deep breath

So far, this has just been a high-level overview

I wanted to show *why* you might want to write functions first

Let's dig into the particulars

# Function components

## Three components

* `formals()`
* `body()`
* `environment()` (we won't focus much on this for now)


![](https://github.com/uo-datasci-specialization/c3-fp-2022/blob/main/static/slides/img/bodyFormals.png?raw=true)

## Function structure

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|1,3|2"
fx_name <- function(formals){
  body
}
```

## Formals = arguments {.smaller}

Formals are the **arguments** supplied to the function

* What's one way to identify the formals for a function - say, `lm()`?

. . .

`?`
: Help documentation!

. . .

Alternative - use a function!

```{r formals-fun}
#| output-location: fragment
formals(lm)
```

## How do you see the body?

* In RStudio: Super (`command` on Mac, `cntrl` on windows) + click!

[demo]{style="color:#009E73;"}

. . .

Or use `body()`

```{r lm-print}
#| output-location: fragment
body(lm)
```

##################################################

## Environment

As I mentioned, we won't focus on this too much, but if you get deep into programming it's pretty important

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
environment(lm)
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

```{r}
double <- function(x) x*2
environment(double)
```

:::

::::

## Lexical Scoping {.smaller}

- How `R` decides where to look for a variable based on where the function was written (defined), not where it is called
- Each function has a "home" environment it searches first, then works outward
- When a function runs and it sees a variable, `R` checks:
    (1) Inside the function, is the variable there?
    (2) If not, it looks in the environment where the function was created
    (3) Then it moves up until it finds the variable or reaches the global environment

## The Lookup Chain

If a variable isn't found inside a function, `R` looks in the environment where the function *was defined*

```{r scope1}
#| output-location: fragment
x <- "global"

f <- function() {
  x  
}

f()
```

. . .

Here: `x` not defined in the function, so `R` looks outward to the global environment

## Local Definitions Win

A local variable can *shadow* any outer variable with the same name

```{r scope2}
x <- "global"

f <- function() {
  x <- "local"   # shadows the global x
  x
}
```

. . .

What will output here?

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
f()   
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
x     
```

. . .

The function `f()` gets its own x

The global `x` is never touched

## Defined > Called {.smaller}

**where defined** matters, not **where called**

```{r scope3}
#| output-location: fragment
x <- "global"

f <- function() x        

g <- function() {
  x <- "inside g"
  f()                    
}
```

. . .

Predict...

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
f()
```

. . .

Straightforward

. . .

What happens here

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
g()
```

. . .

`f()` is **called** in `g()`, but was **defined** in global

`f()` was defined in the global environment

- so it always sees the global x
- even when called from inside `g()`

## Scoping Summary {.smaller}

::: {incremental}
- If an object is found locally
    + `R` uses it
- If an objects is **not** found locally 
    + `R` looks in defining environment
- If an object is called somewhere new
    + `R` still uses defining environment   

:::

. . .

Generally, you won't have to worry too much about this

If you end up with unexpected results, this could be part of why


## Scoping

* Part of what's interesting about these scoping rules is that your functions can, and very often do, depend upon things in your global workspace, or your specific environment
* If this is the case, the function will be a "one-off", and unlikely to be useful in any other script
* Like our `summarize_df()` function depended on the global `smry` list object

. . .

Let's look at a few real examples

## Example 1 {.smaller}

Format tables for the "Schedule" page in course website

```{r extract_grades}
#| eval: false
#| code-line-numbers: "|2|3|20"
tbl_fx <- function(n){
  sched |> # an object in my environment
    slice(n) |> # 11 rows, weeks in quarter
    select(data) |>
    unnest(cols = c(data)) |>
    gt::gt() |>
    fmt_markdown(columns = c(Reading, Slides, Lecture, Assigned, Due)) |>
    sub_missing(
      columns = everything(),
      missing_text = ""
    ) |>
    cols_label(
      Date = md("<i class='far fa-calendar'></i>")
    ) |>
    tab_footnote(
      footnote = paste0("Passcode: ", sched$lecture_passcode[[n]]),
      locations = cells_column_labels(
        columns = Lecture)
    ) |>
    table_style() # another function in my global env!
}
```

## Example 1 {.smaller}

And that function is specific `gt()` formatting

```{r}
#| eval: false
table_style <- function(data) {
  data %>%
    opt_table_lines(extent = "none") %>%
    opt_table_outline(style = "solid", width = px(3), color = "black") %>%
    tab_options(
      heading.border.bottom.color = "black",
      heading.border.bottom.style = "solid",
      table.border.top.color = "black",
      table_body.hlines.color = "black",
      table_body.border.top.color = "black",
      table_body.border.top.style = "solid",
      table_body.border.bottom.color = "black",
      table_body.border.bottom.style = "solid",
      column_labels.border.bottom.color = "black",
      column_labels.border.bottom.style = "solid",
      column_labels.border.top.color = "black",
      column_labels.background.color = "#009E73", # maybe this color is familiar
      table.width = pct(100),
      container.width = pct(100)
    )
}
```

## "#009E73"

```{r}
#| echo: false
scales::show_col("#009E73")
```


## Example 2 {.smaller}

Reading in data

```{r read-data-fun, eval = FALSE}
read_sub_files <- function(filepath) {
  read_csv(filepath) |> 
    mutate(
      content_area = str_extract(
        filepath, "[Ee][Ll][Aa]|[Rr]dg|[Ww]ri|[Mm]ath|[Ss]ci" # unique name of these "filepaths"
      ),
      grade = gsub(".+g(\\d\\d*).+", "\\1", filepath),
      grade = as.numeric(grade)
    ) |> 
    select(content_area, grade, everything()) |> 
    clean_names()
}

ifiles <- map_df(filepaths, read_sub_files)
```

## Simple example

[**Please follow along**]{style="color:#009E73;"}

Pull out specific coefficients

```{r mods}
#| output-location: fragment
mods <- mtcars |>
	group_by(cyl) |>
	nest() |>
	mutate(
	  model = map(
	    data, ~lm(mpg ~ disp + hp + drat, data = .x)
    )
  )
mods
```

## Pull a specific `coef`

Find the solution for one model

```{r coefs}
# pull just the first model
m <- mods$model[[1]]

# extract all coefs
coef(m)
```

. . .

Extract specific coefs

```{r}
coef(m)["disp"]
coef(m)["(Intercept)"]
```

## Challenge

Can you write a function that returns any desired coefficient from an `lm` model?

Try using the code on the previous slide as you guide

`r countdown::countdown(4)`

## Generalize it {.smaller}

. . .

```{r pull-coef-fun}
pull_coef <- function(model, coef_name) {
  coef(model)[coef_name]
}
```

. . .

Check it

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
pull_coef(mods$model[[1]])
```

. . .

Expand it

```{r pull-coef1}
#| output-location: fragment
mods |>
	mutate(intercept = map_dbl(model, pull_coef, "(Intercept)"),
	       disp      = map_dbl(model, pull_coef, "disp"),
	       hp        = map_dbl(model, pull_coef, "hp"),
	       drat      = map_dbl(model, pull_coef, "drat"))
```

## Make it more flexible

Since the intercept is a little difficult to pull out, we could have it return that by default

```{r pull-coef2}
#| output-location: fragment
pull_coef <- function(model, coef_name = "(Intercept)") {
	coef(model)[coef_name]
}

mods |>
	mutate(intercept = map_dbl(model, pull_coef))
```

## Return all coefficients {.smaller}

First, figure it out for a single case

. . .

```{r }
coef(m)
```

This is a named vector

. . .

```{r }
names(coef(m))
```

. . .

Put them in a data frame

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
tibble(
  term = names(coef(m)),
  coefficient = coef(m)
)
```

## Challenge

Can you generalize this to a function?

That is, pull all coefficients and write to a tibble?

`r countdown::countdown(4)`

. . .

```{r pull-coef3}
pull_coef <- function(model) {
	coefs <- coef(model)
	tibble(
	  term = names(coefs),
	  coefficient = coefs
	  )
}
```

## Test it out

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
mods |>
	mutate(coefs = map(model, pull_coef))
```

## Unnest

```{r unnest}
#| output-location: fragment
mods |>
	mutate(coefs = map(model, pull_coef)) |>
	unnest(coefs)
```

## Slightly nicer

```{r unnest-better}
#| code-line-numbers: "|3"
#| output-location: fragment
mods |>
	mutate(coefs = map(model, pull_coef)) |>
	select(cyl, coefs) |>
	unnest(coefs)
```

## Create nice table

```{r unnest-success-table}
#| error: true
#| code-line-numbers: "|5-8"
#| output-location: fragment
mods |>
	mutate(coefs = map(model, pull_coef)) |>
	select(cyl, coefs) |>
	unnest(coefs) |>
	pivot_wider(
	  names_from = "term",
	  values_from = "coefficient"
  ) |> 
  arrange(cyl)
```

# When to write a function?

## Example

```{r df}
set.seed(3000)
df <- tibble::tibble(
  a = rnorm(10, 100, 150),
  b = rnorm(10, 100, 150),
  c = rnorm(10, 100, 150),
  d = rnorm(10, 100, 150)
)

df
```

## Rescale each column to 0-1 {.smaller}

We do this by

- subtracting the `min` value from each observation
- then dividing that by the difference between `max` and `min` values

. . .

For example

```{r }
#| code-line-numbers: "|2|3|4|5"
#| output-location: fragment
tibble(
  v1 = c(3, 4, 5),
  numerator = v1 - 3,
  denominator = 5 - 3,
  scaled = numerator / denominator
)
```

## One column

```{r scale1}
#| output-location: fragment
df |>
	mutate(
	  a = (a - min(a, na.rm = TRUE)) / 
	    (max(a, na.rm = TRUE) - min(a, na.rm = TRUE))
  )
```

## Do it for all columns

```{r scale2}
#| output-location: fragment
df |>
	mutate(
	  a = (a - min(a, na.rm = TRUE)) /
	    (max(a, na.rm = TRUE) - min(a, na.rm = TRUE)),
	  b = (b - min(b, na.rm = TRUE)) /
	    (max(b, na.rm = TRUE) - min(b, na.rm = TRUE)),
	  c = (c - min(c, na.rm = TRUE)) /
	    (max(c, na.rm = TRUE) - min(c, na.rm = TRUE)),
	  d = (d - min(d, na.rm = TRUE)) /
	    (max(d, na.rm = TRUE) - min(d, na.rm = TRUE))
  )
```

##

![](https://media1.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExNmlpeHlvZTEwZXVuZWtkZzE0d2RzZWlyOHpxeGU2Z3F0djZrcmlqZSZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/gsiBBda5eVSIilfGlo/giphy.gif)

## An alternative

What's an alternative we could use *without* writing a function?

. . .

```{r scale3}
#| output-location: fragment
map_df(df, ~(.x - min(.x, na.rm = TRUE)) / 
              (max(.x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))
```

## Another alternative

`across()`

```{r}
df |>
	mutate(
	  across(c(a:d), ~(. - min(., na.rm = TRUE)) / 
	    (max(., na.rm = TRUE) - min(., na.rm = TRUE))
	  )
  )
```


## Another alternative

Write a function

* What are the arguments going to be?
* What will the body be?

. . .

**Arguments**

* One formal argument - A numeric vector to rescale

. . .

**Body**

* You try first

`r countdown::countdown(3)`

## Body

. . .

```{r body-fun}
#| eval: false
(x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / 
  (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
```

. . .

**Create the function**

```{r rescale1}
rescale01 <- function(x) {
  (x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / 
    (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
}
```

## Test it!

```{r test-rescale1}
#| output-location: fragment
rescale01(c(0, 5, 10))
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
rescale01(c(seq(0, 100, 10)))
```

## Make it cleaner

* There's nothing "wrong" with the prior function, but it is a bit hard to read
* How could we make it easier to read?

. . .

- Remove missing data once (rather than every time)
    + Try not to calculate things multiple times!

## A little cleaned up

```{r rescale2}
#| code-line-numbers: "|2"
rescale01b <- function(x) {
	z <- na.omit(x)
	min_z <- min(z)
	max_z <- max(z)

	(z - min_z) / (max_z - min_z)
}
```

. . .

**Test it!**

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
rescale01b(c(0, 5, 10))
```

. . .

```{r test-rescale2}
#| output-location: fragment
rescale01b(c(seq(0, 100, 10)))
```

## Make sure they give the same output

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
identical(rescale01(c(0, 1e5, .01)), rescale01b(c(0, 1e5, 0.01)))
```

. . .

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
rand <- rnorm(1e3)
identical(rescale01(rand), rescale01b(rand))
```

# Conditions

## Getting more complex

What if you want a function to behave differently depending on the input?

. . .

Add conditions!

## Condition

```{r condition-template1}
#| eval: false
function() {
	if (condition) {
  
  # code executed when condition is TRUE
	
	} else {
	  
  # code executed when condition is FALSE
	
	}
}
```


## Many conditions

```{r condition-template2}
#| eval: false

function() {
	if (this) {
  
  # do this
	
	} else if (that) {
  
  # do that
	
	} else {

	# something else
	
	}
}
```

## Easy example

Given a vector, return the mean if it's numeric, and `NULL` otherwise

. . .

```{r mean2}
#| code-line-numbers: "|2-4|5-7|2|3|5|6|8"
mean2 <- function(x) {
	if(is.numeric(x)) {
		out <- mean(x)
	}
	else {
		return(NULL)
	}
  out
}
```

## Test it

```{r mean2-test}
mean2(rnorm(12))
```

. . .

```{r}
mean2(c("a", "b", "c"))
```

## Mean for all numeric columns {.smaller}

* The `mean2` function can be used within a new function to
    + calculate the mean of all numeric columns in a data frame
    + drop all non-numeric columns
* First, let's do it "by hand" 
* Then we'll wrap it in a function

. . .

We'll use `ggplot2::mpg`

```{r }
mpg
```

## Calculate all means

We already have this function

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
means_mpg <- map(mpg, mean2)
means_mpg
```

## Drop non-numeric columns

First identify which are `NULL`

```{r }
#| output-location: fragment
is_null <- map_lgl(means_mpg, is.null)
is_null
```

## Subset

Use the `is_null` vector to subset

```{r }
means_mpg[!is_null]
```

## Transform to a data frame

```{r }
as.data.frame(means_mpg[!is_null])
```

## Challenge

Can you wrap all of the previous into a function?

`r countdown::countdown(5)`

## Solution

. . .

```{r df-mean}
means_df <- function(df) {
	means <- map(df, mean2) # calculate means
	nulls <- map_lgl(means, is.null) # find null values
	means_l <- means[!nulls] # subset list to remove nulls
	
	as.data.frame(means_l) # return a df
}
```

. . .

```{r }
head(mpg)
means_df(mpg)
```

## We have a problem though!

We didn't account for `NA`s

. . .

```{r ozone-means}
head(airquality)
```

. . .

```{r }
means_df(airquality)
```

. . .

How can we fix it?

## Easiest way `...`

**Pass the dots!**

Redefine `mean2`

```{r means2-redefined}
#| code-line-numbers: "|1,3"
mean2 <- function(x, ...) {
	if(is.numeric(x)) {
		mean(x, ...)
	}
	else {
		return()
	}
}
```

## Pass the dots

`(...)` allows a function to accept an arbitrary number of arguments (named or unnamed) and pass them along to another internal function

For example

`my_plot <- function(x, ...) { ggplot(x, ...) }` allows you to pass any `ggplot()` parameters like size=4


## Redefine `means_df`

```{r df-mean-redefined}
#| code-line-numbers: "|1-2"
means_df <- function(df, ...) {
	means <- map(df, mean2, ...) # calculate means
	nulls <- map_lgl(means, is.null) # find null values
	means_l <- means[!nulls] # subset list to remove nulls
	
	as.data.frame(means_l) # return a df
}
```

. . .

```{r}
means_df(airquality)
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
means_df(airquality, na.rm = TRUE)
```

# Functions: Part 2

## Agenda {.smaller}

* Purity (quickly)
* warnings, messages, and errors
    + more function conditionals
* Return values
* Function names

**Learning objectives**

* Understand the concept of purity, and why it is often desirable
    + And be able to define a side effect
* Be able to change the behavior of a function based on the input
* Be able to embed warnings/messages/errors

# Purity

## Purity

A function is pure if:

1. Its output depends **only** on its inputs
2. It produces no **side-effect**

## Side-effects {.smaller}

Side-effects
: any observable changes occurring outside the function's local environment

. . .

- It makes no changes to the "state of the world"
    + this is not a technical term, just a way to think about it

## Side effect examples {.smaller}

:::{.incremental}
- modifying global variables 
    + `<<-`
- console output
    + Printing messages to the console using `print()`, `cat()`, `message()`, `warning()`
- creating plots 
    + `plot()`, `hist()`, `ggplot()`
- saving files 
    + `write.csv()`, `saveRDS()`, `sink()`
- changing the environment
    + `setwd()`, `options()`
- package management
    + loading or attaching packages within a function using `library()` or `require()`
- setting the seed
    + `set.seed()`

:::

# Warnings, messages, and errors

more function conditionals

## Conditionals {.smaller}

Example from an old lab:

*Write a function that takes two vectors of the same length and returns the total number of instances where the value is `NA` for both vectors. For example, given the following two vectors*

```{r vects1}
#| eval: false
c(1, NA, NA, 3, 3, 9, NA)
c(NA, 3, NA, 4, NA, NA, NA)
```

*the function should return a value of `2`, because the vectors are both `NA` at the third and seventh locations. Provide at least one additional test that the function works as expected.*

## How do you *start* to solve this problem?

~~With a function! 🤓~~

Solve it on a test case, then generalize 😎

. . .

Use the vectors to solve!

```{r vects2}
a <- c(1, NA, NA, 3, 3, 9, NA)
b <- c(NA, 3, NA, 4, NA, NA, NA)
```

You try first. See if you can use these vectors to find how many elements are `NA` in both (should be 2)

`r countdown::countdown(4, top = 0)`

## One approach

. . .

```{r}
is.na(a) 
is.na(b)
```

. . .

```{r}
is.na(a) & is.na(b)
```

. . .

```{r}
sum(is.na(a) & is.na(b))
```

. . .

Anyone do anything differently?

## Generalize to function

`r countdown::countdown(3)`

. . .

```{r both_na-fun}
both_na <- function(x, y) {
	sum(is.na(x) & is.na(y))
}
```

## Test it

```{r test-both_na}
both_na(a, b)
both_na(c(a, a), c(b, b))
```

. . .

What happens if not same length?

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
both_na(a, c(b, b))
```

. . .

🤔

## What's going on here? {.smaller}

**Recycling**

* `R` will **recycle** vectors if they are divisible

```{r recycle1}
#| output-location: fragment
data.frame(nums = 1:4,
           letts = c("a", "b"))
```

. . .

* This will not work if they are not divisible

```{r recycle-fail}
#| error: true
data.frame(nums = 1:3,
           lets = c("a", "b"))
```

## Unexpected results

In the `both_na()` function, recycling can lead to unexpected results, as we saw

What should we do?

. . .

Check that they are the same length, return an error if not

## `stop("message")`

Check the lengths and `stop` the evaluation of a function and return an error message `if` lenghts are unequal

. . .

Basic structure

```{r error1a}
#| code-line-numbers: "|1|2,4|3|5"
both_na <- function(x, y) {
	if(condition) {
		stop("message")
	}
	sum(is.na(x) & is.na(y))
}
```

## Challenge

`r countdown::countdown(2)`

- Modify the code below to check that the vectors are of the same length
- Return a *meaningful* error message if not
- Test it out to make sure it works!

```{r error1b}
#| eval: false
both_na <- function(x, y) {
	if(condition) {
		stop("message")
	}
	sum(is.na(x) & is.na(y))
}
```

## Attempt 1

Did yours look something like this?

. . .

```{r error-message1}
#| error: true
both_na <- function(x, y) {
	if(length(x) != length(y)) {
		stop("Vectors are of different lengths")
	}
	sum(is.na(x) & is.na(y))
}
```

. . .

```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
both_na(a, b)
```


```{r}
#| output-location: fragment
#| error: true
both_na(a, c(b, b))
```

## More meaningful error message?

What would make it more meaningful?

. . .

State the lengths of each?

. . .

```{r error-message2}
#| code-line-numbers: "|3-4|5"
both_na <- function(x, y) {
	if(length(x) != length(y)) {
		v_lngths <- paste0("x = ", length(x), 
		                   ", y = ", length(y))
		stop("Vectors are of different lengths: ", v_lngths)
	}
	sum(is.na(x) & is.na(y))
}
```

. . .

```{r}
#| error: true
both_na(a, c(b, b))
```

## `call. = FALSE`

Maybe we don't need/want to echo the function: `Error in function_name():`

Set `call. = FALSE`

```{r }
#| code-line-numbers: "|8"
both_na <- function(x, y) {
	if(length(x) != length(y)) {
		v_lngths <- paste0("x = ", length(x), 
		                   ", y = ", length(y))
		stop(
		  "Vectors are of different lengths:", 
		  v_lngths,
		  call. = FALSE
	  )
	}
	sum(is.na(x) & is.na(y))
}
```

. . .

```{r}
#| error: true
both_na(a, c(b, b))
```

## Clean up

We also can state the lengths on new lines with `\n`

```{r }
#| code-line-numbers: "|4|7"
both_na <- function(x, y) {
	if(length(x) != length(y)) {
		v_lngths <- paste0("x = ", length(x), 
		                   "\n",
		                   "y = ", length(y))
		stop(
		  "Vectors are of different lengths:\n", 
		  v_lngths,
		  call. = FALSE
	  )
	}
	sum(is.na(x) & is.na(y))
}
```

. . .

```{r}
#| error: true
both_na(a, c(b, b))
```

## `stopifnot()` {.smaller}

Quick error messages

* For quick checks, with usually less than optimal messages, use `stopifnot`
* Often useful if the function is just for you

. . .

```{r z-score-stopifnot}
#| code-line-numbers: "|2"
#| output-location: fragment

z_score <- function(x) {
  stopifnot(is.numeric(x))
  x <- x[!is.na(x)]
  (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
}
```

. . .

```{r}
z_score(c(100, 115, 112))
```

. . .

```{r}
#| error: true
z_score(c("a", "b", "c"))
```


## Warnings

If you want to embed a warning, just swap out `stop()` for `warning()`

. . .

Let's build out a warning if the vectors are different lengths, but they **ARE** recyclable

## Modulo operator

`%%` returns the remainder in a division problem

For example:

- `8 %% 2` and `8 %% 4` both return zero (because there is no remainder)
- `7 %% 2` returns 1 
- `7 %% 4` returns 3

## One approach

Note the double condition

```{r error-warn}
#| code-line-numbers: "|2,15,16|8-14|8-10|11-14"
both_na <- function(x, y) {
	if(length(x) != length(y)) {
		lx <- length(x)
		ly <- length(y)
		
		v_lngths <- paste0("x = ", lx, ", y = ", ly)

		if(lx %% ly == 0 | ly %% lx == 0) {
			warning("Vectors were recycled (", v_lngths, ")")
		}
		else {
			stop("Vectors are of different lengths and are not recyclable: ",
			     v_lngths)	
		}
	}
	sum(is.na(x) & is.na(y))
}
```

## Test it {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}

Vectors different lengths but recyclable

```{r test-error-warn}
#| warning: true
#| error: true
#| output-location: fragment
both_na(a, c(b, b))
```

:::

::: {.column width="50%" .fragment}

Vectors different lengths **NOT** recyclable

```{r}
#| warning: true
#| error: true
#| output-location: fragment
both_na(a, c(b, b)[-1])
```

:::

::::

## Refactoring

* Improving the internal structure of your code to make it more readable, maintainable, and efficient, without changing its external behavior
    + changing the internals, but the output stays the same
* Next week, we'll refactor th `both_na` function to make it more readable 

## We doing good?

![](https://media2.giphy.com/media/v1.Y2lkPTc5MGI3NjExYWMyNW4ya2x6d3Q2cGZkZ3A3ZWJ2Z2g1OWh4Z3B5N3J6cXMxeWFiaiZlcD12MV9pbnRlcm5hbF9naWZfYnlfaWQmY3Q9Zw/eL2Y2MDEDSIg2zYujc/giphy.gif)

## How important is this?

* For most of the work you do? *Not very*

* Develop a package? *Very!*

* Develop functions that others use, even if not through a package? *Sort of*

# Return value

## Return values

Thinking more about return values

* By default the function will return the last thing that is evaluated
* Override this behavior with `return()`
* This allows the return of your function to be conditional
* Generally the last thing evaluated should be the "default", or most common return value

## Pop quiz

What will the following return?

Try it yourself

```{r return-nothing}
#| output-location: fragment
add_two <- function(x) {
	result <- x + 2
}
add_two(5)
```

. . .

**Answer**: Nothing! Why?

## Specify the return value

The below are all equivalent, and all result in the same function behavior

```{r}
add_two.1 <- function(x) {
	x + 2
}
```

```{r add_two}
add_two.2 <- function(x) {
	total <- x + 2
	total
}
```

```{r add_two.3}
add_two.3 <- function(x) {
	total <- x + 2
	return(total)
}
```

## When to use `return()`?

Generally reserve `return()` for when you're returning a value prior to the full evaluation of the function

Otherwise, use `add_two.1()` or `add_two.2()` methods from prior slide

# Function names

## Function names

Which of these is most intuitive?

```{r fun-names}
f <- function(x) {
	x <- sort(x)
	data.frame(value = x, 
	           p = ecdf(x)(x))
}

ptile <- function(x) {
	x <- sort(x)
	data.frame(value = x, 
	           ptile = ecdf(x)(x))
}

percentile_df <- function(x) {
	x <- sort(x)
	data.frame(value = x, 
	           percentile = ecdf(x)(x))
}
```

## Function names

Personally, I like name my functions with the prefix `fx_`

This helps me find the function later in my code, especially if I wrote mulitple functions in the script

```{r}
fx_percentile <- function(x) {
	x <- sort(x)
	data.frame(value = x, 
	           percentile = ecdf(x)(x))
}
```


## Output {.smaller}

* The descriptive nature of the output can also help
* Maybe a little too tricky but...

```{r percentile_df}
percentile_df <- function(x) {
	arg <- as.list(match.call()) # returns a list of the arguments
	x <- sort(x)
	d <- data.frame(value = x, 
	                percentile = ecdf(x)(x))
	
	names(d)[1] <- paste0(as.character(arg$x), collapse = "_") # names first column after the argument
	d
}
```

. . .

```{r precentile_df-example}
random_vector <- rnorm(100)
head(percentile_df(random_vector))
```

. . .

```{r}
head(percentile_df(rnorm(50)))
```

## How do we do this?

* I often debug functions and/or figure out how to do something within the function by changing the return value & re-running the function multiple times

[**demo with `percentile_df()`**]{style="color:#009E73;"}

## Thinking about dependencies {.smaller}

* What's the purpose of the function?
  	+ Just your use? Never needed again? 
  	    - Don't worry about it at all
  	+ Mass scale? 
  	    - Worry a fair bit, but make informed decisions
* What's the likelihood of needing to reproduce the results in the future?
  	+ If high, worry more
* Consider using name spacing (`::`)


# Next time

## Before next class

- Readings
    + [AdvR(2e) 6.5-6.8](https://adv-r.hadley.nz/functions.html#lazy-evaluation)
